title: 13 legal startups to watch in 2026, according to investors
excerpt: Legal technology has gone from an investor wasteland to one of the hottest parties in AI. Investors told us which startups they're watching.
content_html:
Artificial intelligence has turned one of Silicon Valley's least fashionable sectors into one of its hottest. Investors are pouring money into legal startups, hoping to find the next breakout winner before everyone else does.

For a long time, building software for lawyers felt like a contrarian bet. Investors saw legal tech as too small a category, with slow-moving customers and long sales cycles.

ChatGPT changed the equation. Sales cycles that once stretched for years began collapsing into weeks, as founders raced to apply frontier models to take on work done by attorneys.

Investors from San Francisco to London are now looking to fill a Harvey-sized hole in their portfolios. Funding to legal startups worldwide reached $2.1 billion in the first half of the year, per Crunchbase data.

Business Insider asked a group of venture capitalists to nominate the year's most promising legal startups valued at less than $1 billion. Here are their picks.

Atticus

Nominated by: Ann Crady Weiss, True Ventures (an investor)

Total funding: more than $100 million, according to the company

What it does: Atticus helps disabled and injured Americans figure out what kind of legal help they need, then connects them with the right lawyer or nonprofit to handle their case.

Why it's promising: Atticus is part of a huge but overlooked corner of the legal market. Government and insurance programs distribute more than $1 trillion to individuals each year, but people often struggle to figure out what aid they qualify for or find a lawyer.

Atticus is making that process easier — and scaling quickly. "I've watched them grow from a small fry to the largest provider of legal help to disabled and injured Americans," said Ann Crady Weiss, who sits on the company's board.

Atticus now serves 115,000 people a year and says it has secured more than $7 billion in lifetime aid for clients.

Courtroom

Nominated by: Helen Min, True Ventures (not an investor)

Total funding: Undisclosed

What it does: Courtroom lets litigation teams test their case against simulated juries and judges. Lawyers can upload case materials and see how different arguments, witnesses, and narratives might land.

Why it's promising: Founder Elizabeth Grabowski Parikh learned how litigation teams build a case while working in-house at a startup and pursuing companies accused of infringing its patents. In June, Courtroom launched from stealth with backing from a notable group that included OpenAI employees and Relativity's venture arm.

Helen Min said Courtroom borrows a playbook already proven in marketing: using simulated audiences to test how a message will go over before it goes live. Here, the audience is made up of digital clones — and the stakes are much higher.

Crosby

Nominated by: Abby Meyers, Bain Capital Ventures (an investor), and Aydin Senkut, Felicis (not an investor)

Total funding: $86 million, according to the company

What it does: Crosby is a law firm that sells basic legal services to other companies, mostly early-stage startups. The company pairs lawyers with software it builds on top of frontier models.

Why it's promising: "Rather than selling customers a tool, Crosby delivers a service" from start to finish, investor Abby Meyers said. That could give Crosby an important advantage. Customers may be less likely to replace Crosby with a product from a major lab or try to build a similar tool themselves.

Crosby also learns from every contract it reviews, Meyers said, creating a pool of proprietary data that could help make its service better over time. The company recently worked with Micro1 to build a benchmark for measuring how well models negotiate contracts.

DeepJudge

Nominated by: Aydin Senkut, Felicis (an investor)

Total funding: $53 million, according to the company

What it does: DeepJudge helps lawyers find out what their firms already know. The software searches across documents, emails, and past work, then connects that knowledge to agents and workflows.

Why it's promising: Rather than competing with the likes of Harvey, DeepJudge is positioning itself as the layer that makes Harvey and other tools smarter about how a particular piece of work actually works. Customers include Freshfields, Holland & Knight, Cozen O'Connor, and Greenberg Traurig.

Felicis and Coatue backed the company's $42 million Series A in November. Investor Aydin Senkut said the three founders, all former Google researchers based in Zurich, represent "exactly the kind of technical bar we look for in a category this competitive."

GC AI

Nominated by: Max Abram, Scale Venture Partners (an investor), and Mallun Yen, Operator Collective (not an investor)

Total funding: $73 million, according to the company

What it does: GC AI speeds up contract work for corporate legal teams by helping them draft agreements, review terms, and pull answers from their documents.

Why it's promising: Tools that make lawyers faster can be a tough sell to firms that bill by the hour. In-house legal teams have the opposite problem: Efficiency is part of the job.

GC AI is built for them. The company says it already has close to 1,900 customers.

Founder Cecilia Ziniti brings credibility from having served as general counsel three times. "GC AI feels shaped by someone who has lived the daily grind of the job," Mallun Yen said. That experience shows in a product that's tailored for in-house lawyers, investor Max Abram said, rather than one originally designed for law firms and later adapted for companies.

Laurel

Nominated by: Miloni Madan Presler, IVP (an investor)

Total funding: $153 million, according to the company

What it does: Laurel automatically tracks how lawyers spend their workdays and turns that activity into timesheets, sparing them from piecing together their billable hours at the end of the week.

Why it's promising: Lawyers hate tracking their time, but law firms depend on those records to get paid. Laurel automates the chore while helping firms bill for work that might otherwise slip through the cracks.

More than 150 professional services firms use the software, including EY, Cozen O'Connor, and Husch Blackwell. Investor Miloni Madan Presler is especially excited about what comes next: Laurel is pushing beyond timekeeping to help firms decide how to price work and staff it.

Learned Hand

Nominated by: Ashley Mayer, Coalition (not an investor), and Zach Posner, The LegalTech Fund (not an investor)

Total funding: $5 million, according to the company

What it does: Learned Hand serves as a digital law clerk, helping judges and court staff review motions, research the law, and prepare rulings more quickly.

Why it's promising: Courts are drowning in paperwork and short on the clerks and research attorneys who help judges work through it. "This problem is only getting worse," Zach Posner said.

Learned Hand is already being used by court systems in 10 states, including the Michigan Supreme Court, and is being tested in Los Angeles County, home to one of the nation's largest court systems. Ashley Mayer called founder Shlomo Klapper, a former Second Circuit clerk and Palantir alum, the right person to build it.

Manifest OS

Nominated by: Max Abram, Scale Venture Partners (not an investor), and Yuanling Yuan, SignalFire (not an investor)

Total funding: $72 million, according to the company

What it does: Manifest is a tech-enabled law firm that recruits lawyers to practice under its banner. The attorneys handle client matters and legal strategy, while Manifest runs the back office and builds software to streamline their work.

Why it's promising: Just as Uber put more drivers on the road by making it easier to earn money behind the wheel, Manifest is trying to help more people get legal services by enabling lawyers to take on more clients. The company started with business immigration and says it has processed more than 3,000 visa cases to date.

Investors are betting it can repeat that playbook in practice areas far beyond immigration. Manifest has "absolutely crushed its first market," Yuanling Yuan said, calling chief executive Dan Mishin "a beast of a founder."

Patlytics

Nominated by: Yuanling Yuan, SignalFire (an investor)

Total funding: $65 million, according to the company

What it does: Patlytics builds software for law firms and in-house legal teams to manage patent work. Its tools cover the full lifecycle, from invention disclosures and filings to portfolio management and litigation.

Why it's promising: As Harvey and Legora battle to become the central workspace for lawyers, another group of startups is going deeper, building software for highly specialized areas of legal work. Patlytics is betting that patent law is one of those areas where a broad tool may not be enough.

That bet appears to be paying off. "The growth has been exceptional," investor Yuanling Yuan said. Patlytics works with nearly half of the 100 highest-grossing law firms in the country, such as Latham & Watkins, Morrison Foerster, and Quinn Emanuel.

Sandstone

Nominated by: Abby Meyers, Bain Capital Ventures (not an investor), and Jimena Nowack, IVP (not an investor)

Total funding: $40 million, according to the company

What it does: Sandstone helps in-house legal departments tame the flood of requests coming their way. Its software connects with a company's existing systems and draws on its contracts and other legal data to draft and review agreements, answer questions, and route matters to the right person.

Why it's promising: Corporate legal teams are under pressure to do more work without adding headcount, making them eager for tools that help them move faster. That makes them a strong market for Sandstone.

Demand is already taking off. Sandstone said revenue grew more than fortyfold over a recent 90-day period, with customers such as Wayfair, Mercury, MasterClass, and ElevenLabs. To keep pace, it raised a $30 million Series A led by Lightspeed in June, just six months after closing a $10 million seed led by Sequoia.

Solve Intelligence

Nominated by: Mallun Yen, Operator Collective (an investor)

Total funding: $55 million, according to the company

What it does: Solve Intelligence helps patent teams draft filings, respond to inquiries, and analyze their portfolios. It aims to become the command center where law firms and clients collaborate across every stage of patent work.

Why it's promising: Patent work has become one of legal tech's newest battlegrounds, thanks to its enormous market and mountains of paperwork. Solve Intelligence is pulling ahead and profitable, said Mallun Yen, who backed the company in a $40 million round in December.

Yen spent two decades as a law firm attorney and general counsel, working with patents from both sides of the table. "I know exactly how little patience this industry has for tools that don't hold up under scrutiny," she said. Solve is the first tool she has seen patent attorneys trust from start to finish.

Soxton

Nominated by: Ashley Mayer, Coalition (an investor)

Total funding: $2.5 million, according to the company

What it does: Soxton is an AI-enabled law firm that helps startups handle the routine legal work of getting a company off the ground.

Why it's promising: Most founders cannot justify hiring an expensive law firm on day one, but relying on ChatGPT for legal advice carries obvious risks. Soxton is trying to fill that gap with fast, affordable help from real lawyers. Founder Logan Brown knows the market firsthand from her time as a Cooley attorney.

Early demand suggests the need is real. More than 800 companies have used Soxton since it launched in December, and its waitlist stretches into the thousands, said Mayer, who backed the company at seed.

Wexler

Nominated by: Zach Posner, The LegalTech Fund (an investor)

Total funding: $7 million, according to the company

What it does: Wexler's software helps litigators find answers in case documents and verify that the evidence supports their arguments. The goal is to let them move faster and build stronger cases.

Why it's promising: Global firms like HSF Kramer, Goodwin Procter, and Clifford Chance have adopted the software, and Wexler says it has cut manual review time by 75% in some cases. The company is now pushing further into the courtroom: Its newest tool can flag false or conflicting testimony in real time during depositions and hearings.

blocks.html_en: Why it's promising: Founder Elizabeth Grabowski Parikh learned how litigation teams build a case while working in-house at a startup and pursuing companies accused of infringing its patents. In June, Courtroom launched from stealth with backing from a notable group that included OpenAI employees and Relativity's venture arm.

sentences.en: Why it's promising: Founder Elizabeth Grabowski Parikh learned how litigation teams build a case while working in-house at a startup and pursuing companies accused of infringing its patents.
sentences.zh: 为什么它有前途：创始人伊丽莎白·格拉博斯基·帕里克(Elizabeth Grabowski Parikh)在一家初创公司内部工作并追查被指控侵犯其专利的公司时，了解了诉讼团队如何立案。

sentences.en: In June, Courtroom launched from stealth with backing from a notable group that included OpenAI employees and Relativity's venture arm.
sentences.zh: 今年 6 月，Courtroom 在包括 OpenAI 员工和 Relativity 风险投资部门在内的知名团体的支持下秘密启动。

blocks.html_en: Helen Min said Courtroom borrows a playbook already proven in marketing: using simulated audiences to test how a message will go over before it goes live. Here, the audience is made up of digital clones — and the stakes are much higher.

sentences.en: Helen Min said Courtroom borrows a playbook already proven in marketing: using simulated audiences to test how a message will go over before it goes live.

sentences.en: Here, the audience is made up of digital clones — and the stakes are much higher.

content_html_zh:
人工智能已将硅谷最不时尚的行业之一变成了最热门的行业之一。 投资者正在向合法的初创公司注入资金，希望能抢在其他人之前找到下一个突破性的赢家。

很长一段时间，为律师开发软件感觉像是一场逆势赌注。 投资者认为法律科技这个类别太小，客户流动缓慢，销售周期长。

ChatGPT 改变了这个方程式。 由于创始人竞相应用前沿模型来承担律师所做的工作，曾经长达数年的销售周期开始缩短为数周。

从旧金山到伦敦的投资者现在都在寻求填补投资组合中哈维规模的漏洞。 根据 Crunchbase 的数据，今年上半年全球合法初创企业的融资额达到 21 亿美元。

《商业内幕》要求一组风险投资家提名年度最有前途、估值低于 10 亿美元的法律初创公司。 这是他们的选择。

阿蒂克斯

提名人：Ann Crady Weiss，True Ventures（投资者）

据该公司称，融资总额：超过 1 亿美元

业务：阿迪克斯帮助残疾人和受伤的美国人弄清楚他们需要什么样的法律帮助，然后为他们联系合适的律师或非营利组织来处理他们的案件。

为什么它有前途：阿蒂克斯是法律市场中一个巨大但被忽视的角落的一部分。 政府和保险计划每年向个人发放超过 1 万亿美元，但人们常常很难弄清楚自己有资格获得哪些援助或寻找律师。

Atticus 正在使这个过程变得更容易，并且可以快速扩展。 该公司董事会成员安·克拉迪·韦斯 (Ann Crady Weiss) 表示：“我见证了他们从一家小公司成长为为美国残疾人和受伤者提供法律帮助的最大提供商。”

Atticus 目前每年为 115,000 人提供服务，并表示已为客户提供了超过 70 亿美元的终身援助。

法庭

提名人：Helen Min，True Ventures（非投资者）

资金总额：未公开

功能：Courtroom 让诉讼团队针对模拟陪审团和法官测试他们的案件。 律师可以上传案件材料，看看不同的论点、证人和叙述可能会如何落地。

为什么它有前途：创始人伊丽莎白·格拉博斯基·帕里克(Elizabeth Grabowski Parikh)在一家初创公司内部工作并追查被指控侵犯其专利的公司时，了解了诉讼团队如何立案。 今年 6 月，Courtroom 在包括 OpenAI 员工和 Relativity 风险投资部门在内的知名团体的支持下秘密启动。

海伦·敏 (Helen Min) 表示，Courtroom 借用了一套已经在营销中得到验证的剧本：使用模拟受众来测试消息在发布前的传播效果。 在这里，观众是由数字克隆人组成的——而且赌注要高得多。

克罗斯比

提名人：Abby Meyers，Bain Capital Ventures（投资者）和 Aydin Senkut，Felicis（非投资者）

据该公司称，总资金：8600万美元

公司业务：Crosby 是一家律师事务所，向其他公司（主要是早期初创公司）出售基本法律服务。 该公司将律师与基于前沿模型构建的软件配对。

为什么它有前途：投资者艾比·迈耶斯 (Abby Meyers) 表示，“克罗斯比不是向客户出售工具，而是自始至终提供服务”。 这可能会给克罗斯比带来重要的优势。 客户可能不太可能用主要实验室的产品取代克罗斯比或尝试自己构建类似的工具。

迈耶斯说，克罗斯比还从它审查的每一份合同中学习，创建一个专有数据池，随着时间的推移，可以帮助改善其服务。 该公司最近与 Micro1 合作建立了一个衡量模型谈判合同效果的基准。

深度法官

提名人：Aydin Senkut，Felicis（投资者）

据该公司称，总资金：5300万美元

用途：DeepJudge 帮助律师找出他们的公司已经知道的内容。 该软件搜索文档、电子邮件和过去的工作，然后将这些知识与代理和工作流程连接起来。

为什么它有前途：DeepJudge 没有与 Harvey 等工具竞争，而是将自己定位为让 Harvey 和其他工具更智能地了解特定工作实际工作方式的层。 客户包括 Freshfields、Holland & Knight、Cozen O'Connor 和 Greenberg Traurig。

11 月份，Felicis 和 Coatue 为该公司 4200 万美元的 A 轮融资提供了支持。 投资者艾丁·森库特 (Aydin Senkut) 表示，三位创始人都是苏黎世的前谷歌研究人员，他们“正是我们在竞争如此激烈的类别中寻找的那种技术酒吧”。

GC AI

提名人：Max Abram，Scale Venture Partners（投资者）和 Mallun Yen，Operator Collective（非投资者）

据该公司称，总资金：7300万美元

用途：GC AI 帮助企业法律团队起草协议、审查条款并从文档中提取答案，从而加快企业法律团队的合同工作速度。

为什么它有希望：对于按小时计费的公司来说，让律师工作更快的工具可能很难推销。 内部法律团队面临相反的问题：效率是工作的一部分。

GC AI 就是为他们打造的。 该公司表示已经拥有近 1,900 名客户。

创始人塞西莉亚·齐尼蒂 (Cecilia Ziniti) 曾三度担任总法律顾问，因此赢得了信誉。 Mallun Yen 表示：“GC AI 感觉是由那些每天都在工作中苦苦挣扎的人塑造的。” 投资者马克斯·艾布拉姆 (Max Abram) 表示，这种经验体现在一款专为内部律师量身定制的产品中，而不是最初为律师事务所设计、后来适用于公司的产品。

劳雷尔

提名人：Miloni Madan Presler，IVP（投资者）

据该公司称，融资总额：1.53 亿美元

它的作用：Laurel 自动跟踪律师如何度过工作日，并将该活动转化为时间表，使他们无需在周末拼凑计费时间。

为什么它有前途：律师讨厌记录他们的时间，但律师事务所依靠这些记录来获得报酬。 劳雷尔将家务活自动化，同时帮助公司为原本可能被漏掉的工作计费。

超过 150 家专业服务公司使用该软件，包括 EY、Cozen O'Connor 和 Husch Blackwell。 投资者米洛尼·马丹·普雷斯勒 (Miloni Madan Presler) 对接下来发生的事情特别兴奋：劳雷尔 (Laurel) 正在超越计时，帮助公司决定如何定价工作和人员配备。

博学之手

提名人：Coalition 的 Ashley Mayer（非投资者）和 LegalTech Fund 的 Zach Posner（非投资者）

据该公司称，总资金：500万美元

业务：Learned Hand 充当数字法律助理，帮助法官和法院工作人员审查动议、研究法律并更快地准备裁决。

为什么它有希望：法院被文书工作淹没，而且缺乏帮助法官处理案件的书记员和研究律师。 “这个问题只会变得更糟，”扎克·波斯纳说。

Learned Hand 已在包括密歇根州最高法院在内的 10 个州的法院系统中使用，并且正在洛杉矶县（美国最大的法院系统之一的所在地）进行测试。 阿什利·梅耶尔 (Ashley Mayer) 称创始人什洛莫·克拉珀 (Shlomo Klapper) 是构建该项目的合适人选，他曾是第二巡回法院的职员，也是 Palantir 的校友。

清单操作系统

提名人：Max Abram，Scale Venture Partners（非投资者）和 Yuanling Yuan，SignalFire（非投资者）

据该公司称，总资金：7200万美元

公司业务：Manifest 是一家科技型律师事务所，招募律师在其旗下执业。 律师负责处理客户事务和法律策略，而 Manifest 负责管理后台并构建软件来简化他们的工作。

为什么它有前途：就像Uber通过让开车赚钱变得更容易而让更多司机上路一样，Manifest也试图通过让律师接待更多客户来帮助更多人获得法律服务。 该公司以商业移民起家，表示迄今为止已处理了 3,000 多起签证案件。

投资者押注它可以在移民以外的实践领域重复这一策略。 袁元岭表示，Manifest“彻底摧毁了它的第一个市场”，并称首席执行官 Dan Mishin 是“一位野兽般的创始人”。

帕特里蒂斯

提名人：袁元岭、SignalFire（投资人）

据该公司称，融资总额：6500万美元

业务：Patlytics 为律师事务所和内部法律团队构建软件来管理专利工作。 其工具涵盖整个生命周期，从发明披露和申请到投资组合管理和诉讼。

为什么它有前途：当 Harvey 和 Legora 努力成为律师的中心工作空间时，另一批初创公司正在更深入地开发，为高度专业化的法律工作领域构建软件。 Patlytics 认为，专利法是单一工具可能还不够的领域之一。

这一赌注似乎得到了回报。 “增长非常出色，”投资者袁元岭表示。 Patlytics 与美国 100 家营业额最高的律师事务所中的近一半合作，例如 Latham & Watkins、Morrison Foerster 和 Quinn Emanuel。

砂岩

提名人：Bain Capital Ventures 的 Abby Meyers（非投资者）和 IVP Jimena Nowack（非投资者）

据该公司称，总资金：4000万美元

业务：Sandstone 帮助内部法律部门应对大量涌来的请求。 其软件与公司现有系统连接，并利用合同和其他法律数据来起草和审查协议、回答问题并将问题转给合适的人。

为什么它有前途：企业法律团队面临着在不增加人员的情况下完成更多工作的压力，这使得他们渴望能够帮助他们更快行动的工具。 这使它们成为砂岩的强大市场。

需求已经开始腾飞。 Sandstone 表示，在最近 90 天内，收入增长了四十多倍，客户包括 Wayfair、Mercury、MasterClass 和 ElevenLabs。 为了跟上步伐，它在 6 月份筹集了由 Lightspeed 领投的 3000 万美元 A 轮融资，而仅仅六个月后，该公司就完成了由红杉资本领投的 1000 万美元种子轮融资。

解决情报

提名人：Mallun Yen，Operator Collective（投资者）

据该公司称，融资总额：5500万美元

业务：Solve Intelligence 帮助专利团队起草申请、回复询问并分析其投资组合。 它旨在成为律师事务所和客户在专利工作各个阶段进行协作的指挥中心。

为什么它有前途：由于其巨大的市场和大量的文书工作，专利工作已成为法律技术的最新战场之一。 去年 12 月为该公司提供 4000 万美元融资的 Mallun Yen 表示，Solve Intelligence 正在领先并实现盈利。

甄子丹在律师事务所担任律师和总法律顾问长达二十年，与双方的专利打交道。 “我清楚地知道这个行业对未经审查的工具没有多少耐心，”她说。 Solve 是她认为专利律师自始至终都信任的第一个工具。

索克斯顿

提名人：Ashley Mayer，联盟（投资者）

据该公司称，总资金：250万美元

公司业务：Soxton 是一家人工智能律师事务所，帮助初创公司处理公司起步的日常法律工作。

为什么它有前途：大多数创始人无法证明在第一天就聘请一家昂贵的律师事务所是合理的，但依赖 ChatGPT 提供法律建议会带来明显的风险。 索克斯顿正试图通过真正的律师提供快速、负担得起的帮助来填补这一空白。 创始人 Logan Brown 从担任库利律师时就对市场有第一手了解。

早期需求表明需求是真实的。 梅耶尔表示，自 12 月推出以来，已有 800 多家公司使用 Soxton，其候补名单已达数千人。梅耶尔曾是该公司的种子投资人。

韦克斯勒

提名人：Zach Posner，The LegalTech Fund（投资者）

据该公司称，总资金：700万美元

用途：Wexler 的软件帮助诉讼律师在案件文件中找到答案，并验证证据是否支持他们的论点。 目标是让他们更快地行动并建立更有力的案例。

为什么它有前途：HSF Kramer、Goodwin Procter 和 Clifford Chance 等跨国公司已经采用了该软件，Wexler 表示，在某些情况下，该软件已将人工审核时间缩短了 75%。 该公司现在正在进一步进军法庭：其最新工具可以在证词和听证会上实时标记虚假或相互矛盾的证词。
